verbatim_text: And with that, I'll jump into some of the future work. So within the next one to two years or so, I want to make FlexAgen real. I want us to go beyond math and code, and I want to actually partner with domain scientists. And this is going to come with a lot of challenges. One of which is that a lot of these scientific domains don't actually have this nice verification property that we had with coding and math. And so we're going to need to figure out if we actually need to find ways to simulate running experiments or these other things. And so this is something developing new post-training algorithms is going to be something we're going to need some guidance from domain scientists for. We're also going to need to find ways to ground reasoning in things that are more in the language of physics and chemistry, and maybe even constrain things. Maybe you shouldn't reason about things in a way that doesn't make physical sense and so on, rather than just open domain language. In the next three years, I'd like to develop on this to produce agents that maybe co-design their own infrastructure and system, and maybe evolve their own infrastructure. The goal of this here would be to make FlexAgen and these scientific research agents like it more usable by scientists who maybe only have a Mac mini in their office and need to run an agent on that. We also need to understand for this, because it has a bunch of challenges, how to train a model to actually introspect and understand how its infrastructure is limited in various ways. And we also need to design agent harnesses that can self-evolve. And then further out in the next five years, I want to work on end-to-end scaling laws for scientific discovery.
